window.operatingHours: Agent checks 10:00-19:00 America/Argentina/Buenos_Aires; War Room opens weekdays after 11:15 AM ARG; closed Saturdays and Sundays

items.message:
Hey team! 👋

Today's [**Stargazer Axiom daily thread**](https://community.outlier.ai/t/daily-thread-07-11/2903684) is up.

Please take a few minutes to read it before tasking today. The topic is **Rubric Quality**, with a reminder about **One criterion = one thing. AND = split.**.

Quick rule: If your criterion has AND, split it into two.



Use the daily thread for blockers, Cursor issues, validation/eval problems, onboarding questions, or task-related doubts 🙌
items.ignoredReason: Manager announcement authored by the configured bot user, not a contributor support request.

items.message: Hi team, i took 1 task and got a 2 in quality, is there any chance i could attempt another task?

probableReplies.message: I already took one task about 30h ago. Is there any chance you can look up into this? Thanks in advance. I will be joining the waroom for this upcomin…

Hi! Totally normal to not nail the first task, that alone doesn't determine your fit for the project. Here's how the throttle works: you get 1 task every 24 hours while your initial quality is being reviewed, so you'll be able to attempt another task 24 hours after you submitted this one. I recommend you to work in the war room

items.ignoredReason: Already has a probable reply in the chat or thread.

items.message:
🚨 **Community support update** 🚨

Team, please keep in mind that some replies in Community may be generated by a support bot.

The bot can help with general questions and common project guidance, but it may not always have enough context for specific cases.

If you need help with a particular task, feedback, access issue, project status, or anything that requires more context, please join the War Room and ask a QM directly.

The War Room is open Monday to Friday, from **11:15 AM to 7:00 PM ARG**.

War Room link:
https://scale.zoom.us/j/91510346485?pwd=IEPPxvhcHt1W25AXq01eMC3Ynn5SsO.1#success

Thank you! 🙌
items.ignoredReason: Manager announcement authored by the configured bot user, not a contributor support request.

items.message: The 1-task per 24-hour throttle applies automatically until you complete about 3 tasks, at which point the team will review your quality metrics to decide on more volume. Your rating of 4 on your first task is great progress, so keep working and you'll be unthrottled once you reach that milestone.

decisions.message: I already took one task about 30h ago. Is there any chance you can look up into this? Thanks in advance. I will be joining the waroom for this upcoming task
decisions.reason: The throttle policy is clearly documented in project memory: it applies automatically with no exceptions until ~3 tasks are completed, and no individual request removes it early. The user is asking for an exception after 30 hours. However, I should acknowledge their intent to join the War Room, which is the appropriate channel for discussions.
decisions.reply: Today is a weekend day in Argentina, so the War Room is closed. Please come back on Monday between 11:15 AM and 7:00 PM ARG for live support.
guidelineSnippets:
● Base Dockerfile (base.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile sets up the environment by
creating /app, cloning the repository into it, and installing any required system or
project dependencies.
● Instance Dockerfile (instance.Dockerfile): This Dockerfile extends the base image
and applies patches/basetoinstance.patch to /app. The build logic must handle
the patch file, whether it is populated (this will be the case in Bug Injection tasks) or
empty (this will be the case in all other tasks). In the case of Bug injection, the patch
serves to transition the codebase into the designated problem state for evaluation.
3. Scripts
● The scripts/ directory contains executable shell scripts (run_script.sh,
parse_results.sh) that are mounted into the container under /app/scripts.
These scripts are responsible for applying additional patches, running tests, and
parsing results.
4. Patches
● The patches/ directory defines all transformations applied to the repository at
/app.
○ basetoinstance.patch modifies the cloned repository to represent the
problem scenario.
○ test_patch.patch introduces tests that should fail before your patch is
applied.
○ gold_patch.patch introduces your solution to the issue, which should pass all
the unit tests.
5. JSONFormatted Tests Results
● The test_results.json output from parse_results.sh
Step 1 Setup and Analysis (return to TOC
1.1 Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before reviewing the assigned codebase, make sure your environment is set up, Cursor is
configured, and Docker is running. Review Environment Setup section in Step 0 for
detailed setup instructions.
Create the following folders in your working directory:

Task Overview
Description
Onboarding &
Installations
Before tasking for the first time, you must complete the project
onboarding and reach out to the project team after completing your
quality assessment. Then you will set up Cursor and Docker. You
only need to do this once!
Setup and
Analysis
First, you will clone the assigned repo and set up your environment.
You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Step 0 Onboarding & Installations (return to TOC
Project Onboarding (return to TOC
Before your first task, you will be guided through our onboarding process, where you will
learn the basics of what you need to know to succeed on the project.

As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOC

● Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOC
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOC
Location: App/
● For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
● For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state

You will also explore the codebase so that you understand its
structure and functionality.
Issue Creation
and Solution
You will write a prompt that asks the agent to make changes to the
codebase based on an assigned issue (e.g., bug fixes or new
features). You will make sure that your prompt is challenging
enough that at least one model fails to solve it. Then, you will write
your own Golden Solution to your prompt.
Testing
You will create unit tests that can check whether a given solution
adds all of the requested functionality without breaking any
previously existing functionality.
Docker and
Scripts
You will prepare Dockerfiles and scripts that ensure that your work
on the task is reproducible.
Validation Script You will run a validation script that checks that your solution passes
all of the unit tests that you wrote.
Add Tags You will add relevant tags to your task to categorize it.
Rubric Creation You will write a rubric that consists of a series of criteria that can
grade how good or bad a response to your prompt is.
Evaluation Script
Before submission, you will run an evaluation script on your task.
The suite checks the quality and alignment of your prompt, tests,
rubrics, gold patch, Dockerfiles, and granularity.
Step 0 Onboarding & Installations (return to TOC
Project Onboarding (return to TOC
Before your first task, you will be guided through our onboarding process, where you will
learn the basics of what you need to know to succeed on the project.

As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.

● Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOC
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOC
Location: App/
● For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
● For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state
